title: The Policy Lifecycle
subtitle: From draft to retirement

hook.metaphor: A policy isn't a monument you carve once and leave forever — it's a living thing with a lifecycle: born (drafted), approved, published, periodically health-checked, and eventually retired when it no longer fits. Treat it as a one-time carving and you end up with a wall of fossils: rules referencing systems, laws, and people that vanished years ago.
hook.scenario: An auditor finds a company's policies still reference a regulation repealed five years ago, a system long decommissioned, and a 'contact the head of department' who left in 2019. Nobody owns reviewing them. The policies exist — but what's gone wrong?

learn: Policies move through a **lifecycle**: **draft → review/approve → publish/communicate → maintain (periodic review) → retire.** Skip any stage and the policy decays. Our fossilised policies skipped 'periodic review' — so they slowly drifted from reality until they were actively misleading.

**Drafting and approval** must involve the right people: the subject-matter owner writes it, relevant stakeholders (legal, risk, affected teams) review it, and an appropriate authority formally **approves** it. An unapproved 'policy' carries no weight; a policy approved without stakeholder input often proves unworkable.
**Publishing and communicating** is its own stage (and its own level next) — a policy that's approved but not actually rolled out to the people it governs isn't really 'live'.

**Periodic review** is the most-skipped, most-important maintenance: every policy needs an owner and a review date (e.g. annually, or when the law/system changes). This is what stops the fossilisation in our scenario — outdated policies aren't just useless, they're *dangerous*, because people may follow rules that are now wrong.

And **retirement** matters too: when a policy is obsolete or superseded, formally withdraw it. Leaving dead policies in the library breeds confusion and contradiction (which is the live-policy you should follow?). A clean lifecycle keeps the rule-set trustworthy.
bridge: So at work, you can tell a managed policy from a fossil by asking: *who owns it, when was it last reviewed, and when is it next due?* Every policy needs an owner and a review cycle — because the world changes (laws, systems, people) and a policy that isn't maintained doesn't just go stale, it starts pointing people in the wrong direction. And retire the dead ones, so the library stays trustworthy.

interactions.title: Order the lifecycle
interactions.prompt: Sort each activity into its stage of the policy lifecycle.

items.label: The owner writes the policy and stakeholders review it
An authority formally signs it off
Rolling it out to the people it governs
An annual review to keep it current

interactions.title: The fossilised policies
interactions.prompt: Policies reference a repealed law, a dead system, and a person gone since 2019, with no review owner. What went wrong?

options.label: Nothing — the policies still exist, so they're fine.
options.feedback: Existing isn't the same as current. Out-of-date policies are worse than none — people may follow rules that are now wrong. The missing piece is ownership and periodic review.

options.label: No periodic review or owner — the policies decayed into being misleading.
options.feedback: Exactly. Every policy needs an owner and a review cycle. Skipping maintenance let them fossilise until they actively misdirect — and obsolete ones should have been retired.

options.label: They just need more policies added on top.
options.feedback: Adding more onto a stale, contradictory base makes it worse. The fix is to maintain (review/update) and retire the dead ones — not to pile on.

teachBack: Explain it to a friend in one line: “A policy is a living thing — drafted, approved, published, periodically reviewed, and eventually retired — so every policy needs an owner and a review date, because an unmaintained policy doesn't just go stale, it starts misdirecting people.”

options: Draft → approve → publish → periodic review → retire
Write once and never touch again
checkpoint.explain: Skipping any stage — especially periodic review — lets policies decay or mislead.

checkpoint.q: Why is periodic review the most-skipped, most-important stage?
options: The world changes, and an unreviewed policy goes stale and starts misdirecting people
checkpoint.explain: Laws, systems, and people change; without an owner and review cycle, policies fossilise and mislead.

options: Formally retired, to avoid confusion and contradiction
checkpoint.explain: Dead policies breed confusion about which rule is live — formal retirement keeps the set trustworthy.
